extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real‑time data exchange for continuous synchronization between the physical building assets and their virtual counterpart, interactive 3‑D visualization, monitoring of system states, execution of control logic, and optimisation of building operations. It supports monitoring and control actions for Facility Managers, device modeling and control‑logic definition for Building Control Engineers, and integration of custom analytics or services for DT Engineers. Services are delivered through automated deployment of DT artifacts, runtime data integration, and modular extension points.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on BIM models enriched with sensor, actuator and controller definitions, model‑driven engineering (MDE) to extract structured representations and generate deployable DT artifacts, an automated deployment pipeline (R2), real‑time data integration via Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto (R3), and a modular architecture with defined extension points for scalable service‑oriented development (R4). The framework uses the Eclipse ecosystem and supports custom plugins developed by DT Engineers.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The solution uses a regular 1‑second heartbeat mechanism to coordinate data exchange between the physical building and its digital twin, providing continuous real‑time synchronization. Measured Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) timeliness of 0.997 indicates a sub‑second latency. Full twin deployment (model checking, parsing, initialization) completes in 14.53 seconds (5.53 s + 8.7 s + 0.3 s).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture adopts a centralized constellation: a single DT Framework hub aggregates multiple device‑level twins (sensors, actuators, controllers) derived from enriched BIM models. Hierarchical relationships follow the BIM hierarchy, and all sub‑components are coordinated through the central hub rather than via decentralized independent twins.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a modular, service‑oriented pattern. Enriched BIM models are transformed via model‑driven engineering into deployable artifacts. Runtime components include Eclipse Hono for device connectivity, Eclipse Ditto as the digital‑twin runtime, and a configurable DT Framework that provides standardized extension points and runtime configuration management. The central hub orchestrates bidirectional data flow, supports plug‑in services, and enables scalable deployment across the building lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability is realized through Eclipse Hono for ingesting device data and Eclipse Ditto for exposing the twin via standard REST/WS APIs. The framework consumes BIM data and offers standardized extension points, allowing external analytics services, enterprise IT systems, or other digital twins to connect. Non‑functional requirements stress scalability, interoperability across heterogeneous systems, and accessibility for users with varying technical expertise.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework uses enriched BIM models (Revit exported to IFC) as the source of geometric models (3‑D building geometry, spatial relationships) and device models (sensors, actuators, controllers) defined through custom Revit families and property sets. From these models the Model‑Driven DT Deployment Framework extracts a structured semantic model (F2) that captures device identifiers (Instance General UUID), IFC type classifications (e.g., IFCLIGHTFIXTURETYPE), and control logic. The extracted model is transformed into deployable DT artifacts that include a geometric representation rendered in Unreal Engine 5, behavioral models encoded in TwinComponent / FeatureParser plugins (e.g., LightTwinComponent reacting to on/off, brightness, colour‑temperature), and data‑flow configurations for real‑time exchange. Data sources are the physical devices themselves; data are ingested via Eclipse Hono (MQTT/AMQP) and persisted in Eclipse Ditto, which also stores the twin state. Model relationships are maintained through the UUID linking BIM objects, TwinComponents, and Ditto devices, providing a unified semantic foundation for synchronization and interactive visualization.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuating elements are modeled in the BIM environment as devices with IFC types such as IFCLIGHTFIXTURETYPE. Each actuator is assigned an Instance General UUID and configured with control parameters (on/off state, brightness level, colour‑temperature) that are exposed as twin properties. Runtime actuation commands are sent from the UE5 twin through the UnrealTwinController, which brokers messages to the corresponding DittoDevice via the DittoController; communication uses Eclipse Hono’s at‑least‑once delivery. The framework enforces safety constraints through Eclipse Ditto policy‑based validation and pre‑execution checks that verify command ranges before they reach the physical device, preventing unsafe operations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are likewise modeled as BIM devices with appropriate IFC classifications and a unique Instance General UUID. Their measurement data (e.g., illumination levels, temperature) are streamed in real time from the physical field devices to the twin via Eclipse Hono and stored in Eclipse Ditto, where policy‑based validation checks unit consistency and value ranges. The deployment employs a 1‑second heartbeat mechanism to transmit sensor updates, ensuring a maximum data‑latency reflected in the ODTE timeliness score of 0.997. Sensor data are made available to the UE5 visualization layer for interactive monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Before deployment the enriched IFC model undergoes automated model checking (average 5.53 s) using a custom IFC model checker that verifies required metadata such as the Instance General UUID. After parsing (8.7 s) the framework validates twin‑device bindings and applies Eclipse Ditto policy‑based validation to enforce correct units and value ranges, providing error detection at the data‑ingress point. Runtime consistency is quantified by the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric, which combines timeliness (0.997), reliability (1.0) and availability (1.0) measured over a 12‑minute window with a 1‑second heartbeat. Fault tolerance is achieved through Eclipse Hono’s at‑least‑once delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, ensuring resilience to network disruptions and enabling corrective replay of missed updates.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical building control system (BCS) devices—sensors and actuators—are modeled in BIM with metadata (tenant, device ID, endpoint addresses). At runtime each device publishes telemetry to Eclipse Hono (the protocol gateway) using Hono‑supported protocols (e.g., MQTT or HTTP). Hono forwards the data to Eclipse Ditto, which stores the twin’s state. The DT framework then obtains the live values from Ditto via a persistent WebSocket connection. An initial snapshot of device values and parameters is also sent to the UE5 visualisation client through an HTTP POST request. Prior to deployment the BIM model is validated, and the Hono/Ditto configurator extracts the required metadata and automatically configures the gateways, ensuring continuous real‑time synchronization between the physical assets and the digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are created by the Facility Manager in the DT UI, transmitted to Ditto over the same WebSocket channel, and then routed by Ditto through Eclipse Hono to the addressed physical device. Commands are encoded as structured JSON payloads describing the desired action (e.g., turn‑on, set‑point change). Ditto validates the command schema before forwarding; Hono handles authentication/authorisation (as defined by its security configuration). The device executes the command and returns an execution acknowledgment, which Ditto receives and propagates back to the DT, allowing the twin state to be updated only after confirmation. This round‑trip ensures command validation, feedback, and conflict‑free operation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The technical network consists of a hub‑spoke architecture: physical devices connect to Eclipse Hono (gateway) via MQTT/HTTP/CoAP; Hono forwards data and commands to Eclipse Ditto (digital‑twin server). Ditto communicates bidirectionally with the UE5 visualization client using a persistent WebSocket connection and with the deployment configurator via HTTP POST for initial state transfer. Real‑time operation implies low‑latency links, though exact bandwidth or latency numbers are not specified. Reliability is achieved through automatic configuration, acknowledgment loops, and Ditto’s state persistence. Security mechanisms are provided by Hono’s built‑in authentication/authorisation, but detailed protocols (e.g., TLS) are not described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework defines a five‑stage lifecycle for a building Digital Twin: (1) Device modelling in BIM – Building Control Engineers create custom Revit families and property sets for sensors, actuators and controllers, assign a unique Instance General UUID and set the appropriate IFC type (e.g., IFCLIGHTFIXTURETYPE). (2) Model checking – an IFC‑based model checker validates that all required metadata is present before the model is uploaded. (3) Semantic model extraction (F2) – model‑to‑model transformations generate a structured, domain‑specific representation from the enriched BIM/IFC file. (4) Automated one‑click deployment (F3) – the extracted model drives a model‑to‑text transformation that produces, configures and launches the DT runtime artifacts with minimal manual effort. (5) Runtime operation – live bidirectional data exchange (F4) via Eclipse Hono and Ditto keeps the physical building and its digital counterpart synchronised; interactive 3D visualisation (F5) in UE5 enables immersive monitoring; modular service integration (F6) and runtime configuration management (F7) allow new analytics or control services to be added or re‑configured without redeploying the whole twin. Governance is split across three roles: Building Control Engineer (modeling and deployment), Facility Manager (monitoring and control) and DT Engineer (service extension).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows a model‑driven engineering (MDE) methodology: custom Revit families are defined, the BIM model is exported to IFC, and a dedicated IFC model checker enforces metadata rules (e.g., presence of Instance General UUID). A transformation pipeline then performs model‑to‑model extraction (semantic model) followed by model‑to‑text generation of deployable artefacts. Deployment is automated through the 'one‑click' mechanism, ensuring reproducible setups and reducing manual intervention (R2). Quality assurance is provided by the pre‑upload model checking and by the deterministic transformation steps. Evolution is managed through a modular architecture with defined extension points (R4), allowing DT Engineers to plug in new services or analytics. Runtime configuration management (F7) lets operators adjust parameters or update interfaces on‑the‑fly, supporting incremental evolution without full redeployment.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The solution complies with BIM industry practices and uses the Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) as the open exchange format for all model data, ensuring interoperability across heterogeneous BIM tools (NF1). Semantic integration is achieved by mapping Revit custom families to IFC types and by extracting domain‑specific models that conform to BIM's object‑based 3D representation standards. Real‑time connectivity leverages Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto, which implement standard IoT messaging patterns (e.g., MQTT with reliable at‑least‑once delivery). The modular service layer follows a standardized interface specification with defined extension points to guarantee that added services remain interoperable with the core DT platform.

Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific

fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].

for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such

Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our

DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization

management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].

data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.
